what_it_does: Explains why a link-enrichment shim must live in the body of ledger items rather than in frontmatter, because the runtime's _render only persists a fixed set of FRONTMATTER_KEYS, and proposes a one-way L2 shim that appends a fenced Dataview inline-field block translating typed links into wikilinks; it also sketches an alternative sidecar-note approach.
observations: _render (cc_board.py:175-178) writes only FRONTMATTER_KEYS and drops any extra frontmatter keys on the next runtime write.
The body round-trips intact unless an edit explicitly passes a new body.
The proposed safe enrichment is a fenced body block %%obsidian-host%% ... %% containing kind:: [[target]] Dataview inline-field wikilinks.
The L2 shim is described as ~40 lines, one-way, idempotent, with a 1:1 name map and no translation table.
An alternative L2 uses sidecar notes (_graph/item-XXX.links.md) with ![[item-XXX]] embeds to keep canonical files byte-identical.
standouts: The core architectural constraint: enrichment cannot survive in frontmatter, so it must go in the body.
Concrete shim design: regenerate a fenced body block of Dataview inline-field wikilinks derived from the links: frontmatter.
A sidecar-note alternative that trades graph cleanliness for source purity.
conventions: Uses a fenced marker block (%%obsidian-host%% ... %%) to isolate generated content.
Preserves link kind as an inline-field key rather than flattening to untyped wikilinks.
concerns: A runtime body edit that overwrites the body would collide with/clear the shim block.
The sidecar alternative creates a parallel note per item and a potentially messier graph.
The graph-edge claim depends on Dataview treating inline-field wikilinks as graph edges, which is tool-specific.
notes: This item argues that link enrichment must be written into the item body as a fenced Dataview block, not frontmatter, because the runtime renderer only preserves known frontmatter keys.
questions: What is the exact relationship between L1 and L2 shims in this system?
How is the shim triggered and how does it detect or refresh stale fenced blocks?
Under what conditions does the runtime pass body during edits, and how is collision with the shim block handled in practice?
Are there existing items with extra frontmatter keys that have already been silently dropped?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it explains why the shim must be thin and live in the body, then proposes concrete implementations.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a stable, non-destructive integration point between the Python ledger runtime and an Obsidian graph view by mapping typed frontmatter links into body-level wikilink inline fields that survive re-rendering.
novelty: The distinct idea is using a fenced, body-appended Dataview inline-field block as a shim layer that survives frontmatter re-rendering, paired with a sidecar-note alternative that leaves canonical files untouched.
contribution: Contributes the constraint analysis that frontmatter keys are volatile while the body is safe, and two concrete shim strategies—an in-body fenced Dataview block and sidecar notes—for bridging typed ledger links to Obsidian's graph without changing the runtime's frontmatter schema.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item explaining that ledger item frontmatter is overwritten by the runtime, so link-enrichment shims must append a fenced Dataview inline-field block to the body, with an optional sidecar-note alternative.
intention_for_embedding: To decide where an Obsidian-graph shim should live so it survives runtime re-renders and preserves link semantics.
